title: Methods
text: In order to examine the relationship between diet and overall health, various packages in both Python and R were used to clean and process the data. The data used in the analysis of this project was found in a paper titled Gut microbiome signatures of vegan, vegetarian and omnivore diets and associated health outcomes across 21,561 individuals. There are 93 samples within the data, each corresponding to a person whose gut microbiome was studied. The data contained here is separated into three .xlsx files.

title: Methods
text:
1 Data Organization
The first file contains ages, diet types, sex, and diet duration. There are also columns containing a HEI Flex score, which is a measure of diet quality, assigned a score ranging from 0 to 100. The data file breaks down each person’s diet into categories, with the first set of categories being adjectives such as sweet foods, savory foods, and so on. Then, the second category is for specific food groups, such as protein, carbohydrates, and whole grains. Each one of these categories is given an individual HEI Flex score, with the HEI Flex column being an average score based on the various diet categories. Additionally, this file contains intake measured in grams per day for each category. The last section of data within this file contains concentrations of various biomarkers, such as cholesterol, triglycerides, and other clinical variables.
The second data file contains taxonomic data about the microbiota within the participants’ gut microbiomes. This data is represented by the unique taxonomy of bacteria found within each person’s gut microbiome, formatted into one cell in the spreadsheet per sample.

title: Methods
text:
2 Data Cleaning and Processing
In order to simplify statistical processing, we combined all three files into one master file, labeled feature_matrix.csv. This is a CSV file for easier processing, as it does not require the additional formatting consideration when imported into Python and R that an .xlsx file requires.
Additionally, the bacterial taxonomic data was formatted into binary data for each sample by creating a function to check whether genomic completeness of a species was greater than 95% with less than 5% genomic contamination. If it met these parameters, a 1 was inserted into the column for that bacterial species within that individual. If it did not meet these parameters, a 0 was inserted, creating binary bacterial columns. This allowed us to integrate the data seamlessly into the analysis, and to quickly find variables or columns rather than searching through three different files. Lastly, this feature matrix was converted into a data frame for the R scripts, as many statistical packages require data frames.

title: Methods
text:
3 Data Analysis
To examine the relationship between variables within the data, various exploratory methods were used. First, the data was used to plot Q-Q plots in R for each category of data. This was done to check how the data was distributed, normally or otherwise. By plotting a straight line on a graph and then plotting data points around that line, it shows exactly how the data is distributed. The closer the points are to the line, the more normally distributed the data is. The patterns of the data around the line also indicate skew when present. This was done by using the qqnorm() function and inputting the different data categories we had. For each category, such as intakes and biomarkers, a Q-Q plot was made to check that the data was distributed normally. Once that was done and the data was found to be mostly normally distributed, it was passed to other analytical methods.
To examine associations between the variables, heatmaps were implemented within R. This was done by using the corrplot package. Heatmaps were used as an exploratory approach to make sure we were capturing as much of the pattern as possible from the data. To begin, feature_matrix.csv was converted to a table, and from that table, columns were selected and appended into lists to be passed to the heatmap. The columns were then passed into the cor() function to map associations, using a pairwise association method (use='pairwise.complete.obs'). This forms the correlation matrix. To create the visualization, the correlation matrix was then passed to corrplot(), where colors and font sizes were adjusted for readability.

title: Methods
text:
4 PCA
Next, a PCA was conducted in R to reduce noise within the large dataset. It was first conducted on the entire dataset, and the resulting biplots and scree plots showed that it did not capture an adequate amount of variance. So, the data was divided into four groups: HEI Flex scores, intakes, biomarkers, and binary bacterial data. These were used in conjunction with feature_matrix.csv for the rest of the analysis.
After conducting PCA on each group separately, much more noise was eliminated. Biplots were generated, and the heaviest groups (those that contributed the most variance) were selected. This is how we selected groups of data to test the hypothesis, noting that cholesterol and savory food groups were among the heaviest contributors.
The PCA was conducted in R by using prcomp(), which decomposes data into principal components (PC1 showing the most variance, PC2 the second most, and so on). A summary of PCA was generated to ensure statistical fit. fviz was used to visualize biplots, and fviz_eig() was used for eigenvalue inspection. Labels were added with labels=TRUE, and then passed to fviz_pca_var() to plot vectors on a coordinate plane. Vectors were color-coded by contribution using col.var='contrib' with gradient.cols=() to produce interpretable contribution gradients.

4.1 PCA biplots



HEI scores biplot


We can observe HEI Flex contributing to the highest amount of variance.



Biomarkers biplot


we can see Ferritin, LDL Cholesterol, and cholesterol contributing most to the variance.



Intakes biplot


we can see savory foods, carbohydrate, and processed foods contributing most to the variance



Bacteria biplot


we can see Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis contributing to most of the variance.

title: Methods
text:
5 Logistic Regression
The logistic regression was done in R, using glmnet to do the regression, and then visualizing it with ggplot2 and dplyr. To finish the analysis, we needed to hone in on exact relationships after looking at associations. Logistic regression was selected because it can handle binary outcomes with continuous numerical data.
The data used in the PCA was imported, since it was already grouped categorically. Similar to the heatmaps, the function glm() is assigned to a variable, and the variable is accessed to view regression output. The regression variables here were simply named logisticmodelx, where x was the iteration number of the model. glmnet uses the format glm(y ~ x1 * x2) for regression on two interacting variables. For our analysis, y was the column of bacterial data targeting one species at a time, while x1 was a specific dietary intake and x2 was the biomarker associated with it. The first iteration modeled the presence of A. hadrus in relation to Serum cholesterol levels associated with the intake of savory foods. Additionally, the entire feature matrix was passed to the glm() function to ensure an accurate fit. A summary of the regression is generated and analyzed to find associations between variables and model significance.
The regressions were visualized with ggplot2 as well. A grid is created first using dplyr, and groups are initialized to find the differences between individuals with high, average, and low cholesterol levels. The average cholesterol level is the mean amount of cholesterol found in individuals in the data, with high cholesterol being one standard deviation above the mean, and low cholesterol being one standard deviation below the mean. The cholesterol variable is converted into a factor for formatting in ggplot2. Within the grid, probabilities are predicted and then assigned for regression modeling.
Finally, to visualize the regression, the grid variable is passed to the ggplot() function, and the variables are labeled. Each line is color-coded by assigning Biomarker_Level to color within the ggplot() function. Labels and legends are then added for readability, and the model statistics are analyzed. Adding theme_minimal() allows for a clean look to the final plot, as well as ylim(0,1), which bounds the y-axis (probabilities) between 0 and 1 so that trends are displayed clearly.

title: Results
text:
The tested groups were identified through PCA. It weighted the heaviest categories as the three bacterial species chosen, Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis. Along with these, cholesterol was among the highest biomarker contributors, and savory foods, vegetables, and fruits were among the highest intake contributors. Using these observations, we plotted interactions between bacteria, biomarkers, and intake amounts using logistic regression models.





Effect of savory food intake on Anaerostipes hadrus with cholesterol interaction


Demonstrates inverse relationship in low cholesterol individuals P = 0.0262



Effect of savory food intake on Ruminococcus bicurculans with cholesterol interaction


Demonstrates inverse relationship in low and average cholesterol individuals P = 0.468



Effect of savory food intake on Alistipes putredinis with cholesterol interaction


Demonstrates inverse relationship between high cholesterol individuals P = 0.432
Overall, logistic regression shows a significant relationship between Anaerostipes hadrus, savory food intake, and Serum cholesterol. Cholesterol and savory food intake showed an inverse relationship with A. hadrus. When cholesterol and savory food intake are both high, the presence of A. hadrus is significantly reduced. This is shown by an interaction coefficient of -4.534764, with a p-value of 0.0266. At a 95% confidence interval, this relationship is statistically significant.
However, as cholesterol decreases and savory food intake increases, the presence of A. hadrus also increases. This is likely due to the fact that A. hadrus helps metabolize cholesterol while producing butyrate, a short-chain fatty acid required in gastrointestinal function. Since it uses cholesterol-associated substrates, it follows that there may be increased presence when cholesterol is present, but when cholesterol is excessive, A. hadrus may no longer thrive.
Out of the three bacterial species identified from PCA, A. hadrus was the only one with a significant relationship with cholesterol and savory foods. This may reflect that Alistipes putredinis and Ruminococcus bicurculans are more specialized microbes, responsible for fiber processing and providing a supplemental relationship to A. hadrus. Because of this, other relationships were examined to identify additional factors not captured in the primary hypothesis.
The relationship between all three microbes and vegetable and fruit intake was tested, as well as the relationship between microbes, protein intake, and cholesterol. All microbes demonstrated extremely small interactions between vegetable and fruit intake, with interaction coefficients not above 0.000001, and large p-values (approximately 0.5 to 0.9).
The final interaction tested was between each bacterium, protein intake, and Serum cholesterol. There was no significant relationship for these variables with A. hadrus or R. bicurculans, but there was a borderline relationship observed for A. putredinis (interaction coefficient -0.002, p = 0.07).
Overall, additional tested interactions did not show robust significance. The results suggest there may be biologically relevant relationships, especially for A. hadrus and A. putredinis, but more research is needed to determine conclusive effects.

text:
The tested groups were identified through PCA. It weighted the heaviest categories as the three bacterial species chosen, Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis. title: Results
text:
2 Discussion

2.1 Overview of key findings
The primary finding of this study is that host metabolic state significantly modifies the relationship between dietary intake and gut microbiome composition. Rather than acting independently, savory food intake and Serum cholesterol levels interact to shape the abundance of key gut microbial taxa. This supports a context-dependent model of diet-microbiome interactions, where metabolic biomarkers influence how dietary exposures translate into microbial outcomes.
The most notable effect was observed in Anaerostipes hadrus, a major butyrate-producing species essential for gut barrier integrity and anti-inflammatory activity. The logistic regression model showed a statistically significant interaction between savory food intake and cholesterol levels (p = 0.0266). While A. hadrus remained relatively stable in individuals with low cholesterol regardless of dietary intake, its predicted probability decreased substantially in high cholesterol individuals as savory food intake increased. This suggests that elevated cholesterol may alter the gut environment in a way that increases microbial sensitivity to dietary stressors.
This aligns with known biology: A. hadrus contributes to short-chain fatty acid production, including butyrate, supporting gut epithelial energy metabolism and intestinal barrier maintenance (Vourakis et al.). A. hadrus helps regulate cholesterol-associated gut metabolism, but when cholesterol is too high, the gut environment may become less stable for this taxon. This pattern suggests that A. hadrus abundance is not solely driven by dietary input, but is modulated by host metabolic context, particularly cholesterol availability.
As for the other two species, R. bicurculans and A. putredinis are more specialized microbiota and appear more bile-resistant, allowing them to persist in harsher conditions compared to A. hadrus. This suggests these taxa occupy ecological niches less sensitive to some dietary and metabolic variation, while still potentially contributing complementary fiber-to-butyrate functions (Parker et al.).


2.2 Microbial specialization and ecological shifts
A comparison across taxa highlights clear differences in ecological response. Ruminococcus bicurculans, a fiber-degrading bacterium, showed no significant interaction effect, suggesting relative stability across dietary and metabolic conditions in this dataset. In contrast, Alistipes putredinis displayed an opposite trend, with increased predicted abundance in high-cholesterol, high-savory conditions (Parker et al.).
These contrasting patterns suggest a shift in microbial ecology driven by host metabolic state. A possible explanation involves bile acid metabolism. Elevated cholesterol is associated with increased bile acid production, which can act as a selective antimicrobial agent. This environment may not favor sensitive fiber-fermenting bacteria such as A. hadrus while favoring bile-resistant species such as A. putredinis. The resulting shift reflects a transition from fiber-degrading microbial communities toward bile-tolerant taxa adapted to high-fat, metabolically stressful environments, consistent with diet-associated dysbiosis (Zeng et al., 2017).


2.3 Biological interpretation
These findings align with existing research on short-chain fatty acids and gut health. Butyrate-producing bacteria such as A. hadrus contribute to serving as an energy source for colon epithelial cells and maintaining gut barrier function. Their reduction may therefore have downstream effects on inflammation and metabolic regulation. The observed interaction suggests that cholesterol does not only act as a passive biomarker but may actively shape the gut environment, modifying how dietary inputs influence microbial survival and function (Ridlon et al., 2014).


2.4 New knowledge and implications
This study introduces an interaction-centered framework for understanding diet-microbiome relationships. Rather than treating diet as a universal predictor of microbial composition, the results demonstrate that host metabolic state functions as a key modifier. Specifically, individuals with elevated cholesterol may be more susceptible to diet-associated reductions in beneficial butyrate-producing bacteria. This has important implications for personalized nutrition. The same dietary pattern may produce different microbiome outcomes depending on an individual’s metabolic profile, suggesting that dietary recommendations may need to account for baseline biomarkers such as cholesterol.

title: Results
text:
3 Limitations
This study is cross-sectional, meaning that causal relationships cannot be established. While the models identify strong associations and interactions, they cannot determine whether elevated cholesterol directly causes microbial changes or whether microbial shifts contribute to altered cholesterol levels. Prior literature has shown that diet-microbiome associations can be influenced by unmeasured confounders such as host genetics, sequencing batch effects, and lifestyle variation (Falony et al., 2016). Initial quality control analyses revealed residual structure in the data, suggesting that unmeasured variables may still be influencing variance in the model.

title: Results
text:
4 Future work
Future studies should use longitudinal and mechanistic designs to better establish causality. Tracking individuals over time would help determine whether changes in cholesterol precede microbial shifts. Intervention studies could test whether altering host metabolic state, such as Serum cholesterol levels, restores beneficial butyrate-producing taxa. Expanding this work using shotgun metagenomic sequencing would allow functional pathway profiling rather than relying only on taxonomic presence. Finally, experimental validation in controlled in vitro gut models could help confirm whether cholesterol or bile concentrations directly suppress sensitive fiber-fermenting bacteria.

title: Results
text:
5 Conclusion
This study demonstrates that the relationship between dietary intake and gut microbiome composition is not a simple linear association, but rather a dependent interaction shaped by host metabolic state. Using an integrated bioinformatics pipeline incorporating principal component analysis (PCA) and logistic interaction modeling, we identified Serum cholesterol as a key modifier of microbial responses to dietary exposures.
The most notable finding was the statistically significant interaction observed for Anaerostipes hadrus (p = 0.0266), suggesting that elevated savory food intake is associated with reduced probability of beneficial butyrate-producing taxa in the presence of high cholesterol. Given the critical role of butyrate-producing and fiber-degrading bacteria in maintaining intestinal homeostasis, these findings provide a potential mechanistic explanation for how western dietary patterns may contribute to gut dysbiosis and subsequent metabolic dysfunction (Koh et al., 2016; Louis and Flint, 2017).
Additionally, differences observed across taxa, including Ruminococcus bicurculans and Alistipes putredinis, highlight the importance of microbial specialization and ecological adaptation within the gut environment. Collectively, these results support a context-dependent model of diet-microbiome interactions, in which host biomarkers help shape microbial resilience and susceptibility. The broader implication of this work is the potential value of personalized nutrition approaches that incorporate host metabolic biomarkers alongside dietary recommendations.
Rather than assuming uniform dietary effects across individuals, these findings suggest that metabolic context should be considered when evaluating microbiome-associated disease risk and designing targeted interventions. Future longitudinal and mechanistic studies will be necessary to validate these relationships and further investigate the role of cholesterol-mediated microbial shifts in cardiometabolic health.

title: Results
text:

title: Microbiome x Cardiometabolic Health
text:
Bioinformatics Capstone
n = 92 samples. Host phenotyping includes age, sex, BMI, and clinical biomarkers. ZOE PREDICT 1 (UK subset cohort).

title: Microbiome x Cardiometabolic Health
text:
1 Abstract
Humans have co-evolved with a complex internal ecosystem of microbiota, functioning less as isolated organisms and more as symbiotic vessels for trillions of microbial cells. While diets high in savory, energy-dense foods, and elevated Serum cholesterol are well-established risk factors for cardiometabolic disease, the precise role of the gut microbiome as an intermediary remains incompletely understood, particularly in the context of how host metabolic state modifies microbial responses to diet. We tested the hypothesis that the interaction between savory food intake and Serum cholesterol levels predicts the depletion of beneficial butyrate-producing taxa, thereby providing a mechanistic link to diet-induced dysbiosis. Specifically, we tested whether these effects could be observed in key butyrate- and fiber processing taxa, including Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis, and whether this relationship depends on host metabolic context.
A cohort (N = 92) was combined with public metagenomic datasets. We implemented an integrated analytical pipeline incorporating principal component analysis (PCA), logistic regression models of microbial presence, and adjustment for age, sex, and BMI, with multiple testing addressed using Benjamini-Hochberg false discovery rate correction. Our PCA, anchored by dietary indices including Healthy Eating Index (HEI) and Flex scores, identified savory intake, carbohydrates, cholesterol, LDL, ferritin, and creatine as dominant contributors, alongside the selected microbial taxa, supporting their inclusion as key features for downstream analysis. Logistic regression revealed evidence of associations for savory intake (p = 0.0495) and cholesterol (p = 0.0183), as well as a statistically significant interaction between these variables (p = 0.0266). The negative interaction coefficient indicated that increasing cholesterol reduced and could ultimately reverse the relationship between savory intake and microbial presence, possibly suggesting a conditional and non-linear relationship between diet and microbiome composition.
These findings suggest that the microbiome functions as a context-dependent mediator of diet-health relationships, with host metabolic state shaping how dietary factors influence microbial composition. This interaction-based framework provides a mechanistic explanation for diet-associated dysbiosis and identifies specific taxa as candidates for future validation in larger, longitudinal studies and potential targets for microbiome informed dietary interventions.

title: Microbiome x Cardiometabolic Health
text:
2 Introduction
Diet is one of the most powerful environmental determinants of human health, with strong evidence linking dietary patterns and metabolic biomarkers to cardiometabolic disease risk. In particular, diets high in savory, energy-dense foods, alongside elevated Serum cholesterol levels, have consistently been associated with increased incidence of metabolic dysfunction, inflammation, and long-term disease outcomes (Hays et al., 2024). However, the biological mechanisms that connect these risk factors to disease progression remain incompletely understood. Recently the gut microbiome has emerged as a key intermediary between diet and host physiology (Hays et al., 2024). Rather than being just a passive group of microorganisms, the gut microbiota actively breaks down the food we eat and produces vital compounds that affect energy use, immune health, and overall metabolism (Portincasa et al., 2021). One of the most important of these processes is the production of short-chain fatty acids (SCFAs), especially butyrate, which plays a key role in maintaining gut health. The depletion of butyrate-producing and fiber-degrading bacteria, including Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis, has been associated with gut dysbiosis and adverse health outcomes (Hays et al., 2024).
Short chain fatty acids (SCFA) are very important in maintaining host symbiosis, as well as directly contributing to colon health (Tan et al.). Butyrate is one of these SCFAs, and is very important in overall health. It breaks down cholesterol within the body, is very important in maintaining homeostasis, as well as nourishing the colon (Snodgrass et al.). Most SCFAs are absorbed directly into the intestines, leaving the colon without any. One of the many reasons that microbiota within the gut are so beneficial is that they produce SCFAs, supplying the colon with an adequate amount of them to function. Without these microbiota, overall health would be greatly impacted, and would negatively impact immune responses, as well as neurological function through the gut-brain axis (Snodgrass et al.). This highlights these taxa as keystone species within the gut ecosystem, due to their central role in maintaining host health.
Anaerostipes hadrus is a member of the Lachnospiraceae family that incorporates SCFAs into butyrate, which aids in cell barrier maintenance and immune responses (Herold et al.). Additionally, the presence of A. hadrus consumes nutrients often used by members of the Enterobactericeae family, and organisms produce pathogen-inhibiting SCFAs (SCFAs other than butyrate) providing resistance against colonization by its members (Herold et al.). Some Ruminococcus species, such as R. bicurculans, are also from the family Lachnospiraceae, but those that are not are from the Oscillospiraceae family that shares many properties with Lachnospiraceae family, being non-motile, gram-positive obligate anaerobes (Valentino et al.). Excessive presence of certain species can cause inflammation, though others are largely beneficial for their ability to hydrolyze resistant starches, N- and O-glycans in the case of Lachnospiraceae species and fiber in the case of Oscillospiraceae (Valentino et al.). Alistipes putredinis is a gram-negative anaerobe recently reclassified from the Bacteroides genus after RNA sequencing (Parker et al.). A. putredinis relationship to overall health is not as clear as A. hadrus or R. bicurculans, but it appears that either excesses or deficiencies of A. putredinis are correlated with disease states, and healthy individuals have a moderate amount of A. putredinis in the gut (Parker et al.).
Despite butyrate’s well-established biological importance, most studies have looked at diet-microbiome relationships and cholesterol-microbiome relationships separately. This misses an important possibility that a person’s metabolic state may change how diet affects the gut microbiome. In other words, dietary effects on gut bacteria may not be the same for everyone, instead they may depend on factors like cholesterol levels. This idea where host conditions influence how diet shapes microbial composition is still not well studied, especially for key butyrate-producing bacteria. To effectively address this gap we investigated whether the interaction between savory food intake and Serum cholesterol levels predicts the presence of key beneficial gut microbes. We focused on A. hadrus, R. bicurculans, and A. putredinis as representative taxa involved in fiber metabolism and butyrate production.

Hypothesis: If there is a significant interaction between savory food intake and serum cholesterol levels, then this interaction will predict the depletion of beneficial butyrate-producing microbial taxa (specifically Anaerostipes hadrus, Ruminococcus bicurculans, and Alistipes putredinis), thereby providing a mechanistic link to diet-induced dysbiosis.

To test this hypothesis, we used an integrated analytical framework combining principal component analysis (PCA), logistic regression with interaction terms, and linear modeling of clinical biomarkers like cholesterol, ferritin, and creatine. By integrating dietary indices (including the Healthy Eating Index and Flex scores), metabolic biomarkers, and microbial composition data from a cohort (N = 92) and public metagenomic datasets, this study aims to move beyond simple associations and toward a mechanistic understanding of how diet and host metabolism jointly shape the gut microbiome.
